entry.title: Components of a Time Machine
entry.content:
There is this spectacular place in cities, where it takes you on a journey to another spot in the world, or invites you on a time travel. If you are feeling interested in this magical journey or just simply lacking ideas on how to spend your next weekend, open your maps and plan a visit to a museum!

Museum is a collection of arts, culture and history. Displayed works often include historical artifacts, therefore it preserves cultural heritage and historical narratives. Some museums are also intended for educational purposes, such as natural and science museums. Museums are almost a must-see in every city I visit. As one of my favorite places, I believe they are where we can learn the most about a city and showcase a large portion of its beauty. Especially in European countries, they demonstrate their artistic commitments through museums, such as the Louvre Museum and British Museum. Museum is a depository of arts, from the inner displays to every block of construction.

Design Features

Note that museums not only serve for the people and the city, but also the artifacts, reciprocating the needs of educators, researchers and artists. The planning must consider an interesting layout for the displayed collections and a fluent circulation through spaces that enhance interaction between the exhibits and visitors, and spaces for wander and immersion. To achieve such purposes, the design of museums must follow some general guidelines (Walshaw):

1. Accessibility
Museum, which welcomes visitors disregarding age and cultural background, must provide equal and proper accessibility to all. Every corner of the building should be able to accommodate any kind of visitors in the same routes, with and without disabilities. Disabled facilities must be constructed on every floor. Lifts should be paired with staircases (Walshaw).The planning of the museum must comprehensively consider how it should be fairly accessible and safe to all.

2. Display and Communication
With current advancement in technology of visualization, there are more options to be considered regarding how to foster interaction between exhibits and visitors. Most museums enforce no-touch policy, but there are numerous mediums to enhance communication between collections and visitors. These mediums may include screens, graphic displays, laser projections and speakers. They are tools that allow visitors to explore more about the topic while creating an atmosphere and immersing themselves in the artistic or historical mood. Most museums also feature theaters where visitors can delve deeper into specific subjects through educational films and presentations.

Shot in Museum of Fine Arts, Houston

3. Environment
Museum collections are very sensitive to the conditions of surroundings, including humidity and temperature. For example, oil paintings should be preserved under high humidity and avoid direct sunlight. Some wax sculptures must be preserved in colder room temperature, 10-20 degree celsius (Burke). Museums must be carefully designed with proper climate conditions for both display and storage of collections to protect and preserve artifacts.

The utilization of light highlights the texture, color and shape of exhibits for finer observation. For example, narrow beam accent lights sheds high illuminance, focusing the center attention of a space. Wall washing light is used to illuminate larger artworks by using wide beam lights to broadly spread across a wall. This kind of lighting often comes with larger exhibits, which light covers on the entire surface, ensuring all details are visible. Wall washing light also makes the room more spacious. With uniform distribution, the room will also feel more spacious, open and inviting (Sylvania). This lighting eliminates contrasts between highlights and shadows, therefore offering a more complemented view of the exhibit. With numerous different types of lights intended for varying purposes, it is crucial to deliberately plan the arrangement of lights as it impacts both the protection of exhibits and the experience and mood of visitors.

4. Circulation
Museums have the power to tell stories by showing the past. This means that sections of the story must be arranged in a correct order for visitors to understand.The exhibition hall of the museum must be designed in a way that allows visitors to travel to any corner and lead them to a specific route that provides the best experience. The entrance must provide a clear guidance to the location of each section as well as service spots. Signage is used for visitors to identify their priorities and what they are more inclined to see.

The circulation of the museum could be in linear form, where there is a clear starting point and ending point. Visitors do not pass through anywhere repetitively throughout the route. The circulation could also be in loop form, where visitors arrive back to the starting point after finishing the route. Other options also include the division of room sections based on particular themes. The planning of circulation is a decisive factor that directly impacts the experience of visitors. A successful circulation should create a step-by-step guide that leads visitors through each section of the exhibition being connected and concentrated on the whole process without feeling skippy. I often find it interesting that the planar layout would always lead me on an arranged order of exhibits based on a storyline. While I am immersed on the beauty of displayed artifacts, I am unintentionally following the route suggested by the designer. What I like about visiting museums is how they are designed in a way that allows me to only concentrate on the appreciation of arts, culture and history, where my mind escapes the reality and loses the focus on what is happening around me.

5. Lighting
Museum lighting is an extremely complex technical work (to the extent that I might write another article focusing on it in the future). Most of the time, only artificial light is used inside the museum and on displayed artifacts. The UV of sunlights and traditional lamps can damage artifacts very quickly, therefore LED is a more ideal light source as it does not create IR and UV (Sylvania). Different kinds of materials of the exhibits also have varying sensitivity to light. Textiles, furs, old photographs, watercolors and stamps are very sensitive to light, prefer 50 lux. Stone ceramic, glass and metal are less sensitive to light, therefore can endure up to 300 lux (Sylvania).

Shot in Shanghai Dongyi Art Museum, Shanghai

The utilization of light highlights the texture, color and shape of exhibits for finer observation. For example, narrow beam accent lights sheds high illuminance, focusing the center attention of a space. Wall washing light is used to illuminate larger artworks by using wide beam lights to broadly spread across a wall. This kind of lighting often comes with larger exhibits, which light covers on the entire surface, ensuring all details are visible. Wall washing light also makes the room more spacious. With uniform distribution, the room will also feel more spacious, open and inviting (Sylvania). This lighting eliminates contrasts between highlights and shadows, therefore offering a more complemented view of the exhibit. With numerous different types of lights intended for varying purposes, it is crucial to deliberately plan the arrangement of lights as it impacts both the protection of exhibits and the experience and mood of visitors.

Uniqueness of Design

Internal planning is indeed the core of museum design, but we can see that a lot of thoughts are also endowed in the exterior design. The external architecture design of museums may be symbolic, reflecting the theme and mission of the museum. To exemplify, the design of the Modern Museum of Art in New York appears in a contemporary architectural design structure, with glasses as the construction material covering a large area of the building. The urban-style building expresses similar modernity vibes as the 20th–21st century collections of paintings, sculptures, and photographs displayed. On the other hand, the Smithsonian National Museum of Natural History is close to a neoclassical architecture, built with white bricks. The museum has a large,impressive central dome that dominates its silhouette. The cracks in the bricks exude a sense of nostalgic elegance. This matches the ancient fossils exhibited inside, taking visitors back to the Mesozoic era. The exterior of the museum has an essential role in creating the imaginary world and its correlating atmosphere, starting from the outside of the entrance. The appearance of the museum is a sign that informs visitors about the journey that they are about to embark on.

The structure's appearance also has a significant impact on the experience of visitors. The aesthetic appeal and thoughtful design of the building enhance the memorable and inspiring adventure through its halls. Just as what one of the architects, Richard Meier, describe in design philosophy of his team, "Through their architecture — the shaping of space, the control of light, the organization of program, the pattern of circulation — they should create an ambience
congenial to contemplative and creative use and the reinforcement of aesthetic values. In this way those who come to the complex will benefit from their
experience not only of the art displayed and conserved but of the architecture as well." *(Mieer and Williams) *A museum is a place filled with the essence of art and history, from the interior to the exterior. Only a visually stunning and thoughtfully designed outer shell can harmonize with the expressive ambiance within.

Shot in Suzhou Museum, Suzhou

Moreever, my perception is, conceiving a unique architectural design creates a focal point, a source of local pride in a community, and focuses attention on historical artifacts and artwork collections. History and arts are invaluable footprints of humanity that deserve to be showcased under a brighter spotlight. While a museum's unique design attracts visitors to appreciate its architectural beauty, it simultaneously highlights the stories within. In this way, architecture acts as a bridge that introduces the cultural, historical, and educational narratives housed inside. It invites curiosity and engagement, drawing people in not just to admire the structure itself, but to explore the rich collections and exhibits it shelters.

“Space will only have a life when people enter it. So the important role architecture can play, and that space plays within that architecture, is to encourage an interaction between people, between people and the ideas being presented in the paintings and sculpture, and most importantly between people themselves.”--Tadao Ando

Museums transcend being merely spaces to display collections. The layout of spaces, the travel of light, and the selection of materials greatly influence the perception and interaction between visitors and exhibits. Each carefully planned component plays a crucial role in creating an atmosphere that fosters connection and communication between individuals and collections, thereby enriching our appreciation of historical and cultural heritage. Ultimately, museums serve to preserve history, art, and culture, providing an immersive experience akin to time travel. By analyzing the meticulous choices in interior design and unique architectural features, we gain insights into how space shapes our understanding and appreciation of art and history.

Works Cited
Burke, Danielle. “Waxwork Wonders: Restoring and Preserving Unique Wax Sculptures.” Fine Art Restoration Company, 20 Aug. 2021, fineart-restoration.co.uk/news/ wax-artwork-restoration/. Accessed 26 July 2024.

Mieer, Richard, and Harold Williams M. The Getty Center Design Process. Edited by Gloria Gerace, The J.Paul Getty Trust, 1998, www.getty.edu/publications/ resources/virtuallibrary/0892362103.pdf.

“Museum Architecture: Purpose, Types, and Design.” AccessDoorsAndPanels, 2 Mar. 2022, www.accessdoorsandpanels.com/blog/museum-architecture-purpose-types-and-design/.

Sylvania, Feilo. LIGHTING for MUSEUMS and GALLERIES. 2015.

Walshaw, Emma. “An Architect’s Guide To: Museum Planning.” Journal, 7 Mar. 2022, architizer.com/blog/practice/details/architects-guide-museum-planning/.

entry.title: Gardens: What's the Difference?
entry.content:
“When we are surrounded by nature, a feeling of comfort comes over us, and our bodies become relaxed. Most of us would intuitively recognize this feeling, but until recently we haven’t had any evidence to prove it.” --Yoshigumi Miyazaki

Gardens have long been an expression of culture, philosophy, and artistry in numerous countries around the globe. In Asia, gardens are especially a representation of national spirit and cultural heritage. Several gardens are considered significant historic landscapes and architecture, part of the World Cultural Heritage. Chinese gardens and Japanese gardens are two popular types of gardens known for their charming and elegant designs that create a scaled-down world of nature.

Chinese and Japanese gardens are inspired by philosophy and religion.The use of water,plants, and rocks expresses the infinite meaning of nature in a symbolic way while allowing adventurers to release their stress and improve their concentration with the power of being surrounded by the pleasantness of nature. Both gardens demonstrate the concept of “borrowed scene,” which utilizes objects from far and near to create pleasing images from any spot on any angle of the garden and express continuity. The principles of aesthetics in the two gardens are to imitate nature and create miniature landscapes. Although both types of gardens share similar elements, some defining features and slight differences in details shape Japanese and Chinese gardens into two distinctive styles.

Japanese Gardens

Early Japanese gardens were inspired by Chinese gardens to a very large extent. Philosophical and spiritual elements, together with concepts of Buddhism and Taoism introduced from China to Japan in the 6th century, formed the initial aesthetic principles of Japanese gardens.

Architecture in Japanese gardens emphasizes minimalism, often uses very few colors, which are less vibrant, in a gray tone, and focuses on presenting the natural beauty of the structure and materials. The choice of materials focuses on simplicity, natural patterns, and texture. These choices are reflected on stone doors, suikinkutsu (water basins), and lanterns, which are regular elements that add harmony and balance to the garden. Japanese gardens may have chashitsu (tea houses), meditation halls, or sitting areas, but they are few in number and have a less diverse style of appearance. These buildings are mostly built with wood due to its abundance as a resource and also because of the country’s religion, Shinto, which views trees as divine; even after being cut into wood, they are still living beings.

Shot in Japanese Garden, Portland, during my summer break, 2024

Water bodies are exquisitely compacted, such as small streams and ponds, focusing on presenting the sense of flow and liveliness of water. Water flows with the wind or elevation throughout the garden. Streams, fountains, and water sounds create a calm vibe and represent renewal and continuity. This is an important element in both Japanese and Chinese gardens, but what I often find in Japanese gardens but not in Chinese gardens is the use of inconspicuous small streams that flow in corners throughout the garden. Japanese gardens do not emphasize the existence of water due to the geographic location of Japan as an archipelagic country and therefore have a greater focus on and fear of water. This can be seen in the use of sand and pebble stones in Karesansui, formed in the shapes of waves to represent water and islands. 

Shot in Japanese Garden, Portland

While designers pay great attention to the overall look of the landscape in Chinese gardens, more effort is spent on the details. Each placement of the object should be deliberately designed. For example, Japanese gardens often show their essence of beauty through rocks. In fact, the selection and placement of rocks and stones is a type of art that often contains specific rules and meanings. For instance, one important element, Karesansui, is a type of Japanese garden style and miniature landscaping consisting of fine sand and stones that are designed carefully in detail. It is a common scene in Japanese gardens that drives visitor to immersion throug tht art of stone placement. The detailing of Japanese gardens is the finishing touch, and the attention paid to the smallest things, such as a branch or mosses on a stone, is extremely important. The level of detail in garden construction and the positioning of objects are especially reflected in patterns formed from stones or inconspicuous illustrations curved on rocks.

Shot in Japanese Garden, Portland

Japanese gardens are very much inspired by Zen, a Japanese sect of Mahayana Buddhism. The belief of Zen is “elimination,” eliminating possessiveness, utilitarian thinking, and obsession with the past, and looking towards ourselves to reflect our real individuality. Inspired by Zen, the overall style of Japanese gardens emphasizes simplicity, serenity, elegance, and contemplation of natural landscapes.

Chinese Gardens

Chinese gardens are significant pieces of Chinese art and culture. The purpose of gardens changes from era to era, and designs also evolve throughout history. Starting in the Han dynasty, gardens were typically built in abodes for emperors to rest in the beauty of natural landscapes. These gardens eventually became popular tourist attractions. Today, we can still see traditional Chinese gardens in the homes of some residents.

One striking difference between the two types of gardens is that Chinese gardens typically have more buildings, such as different rooms to study, read, and entertain, and a diverse type of architecture, including pavilions, pagodas, gazebos, and small houses to break up sections in the garden, allowing each window and angle to show a distinct view from within. The Moon Gate is also a distinctive architectural feature of Chinese gardens, in which we cannot see any other types of gardens. Additionally, these architectures are usually more glamorous, with rich ornaments and colors. Unlike Japanese temples and tea houses that have straight and overhanging roofs, Chinese gardens tend to have eccentric, sloping rooflines. In general, structures in Chinese gardens are mostly round and use mostly curved lines. For example, the roofs of temples and pavilions show more sinuous variations of lines, like a dragon. From a distance, it looks like a blooming, vibrant flower, standing proudly with its head held high in the garden, as if everything around it is foil. On the other hand, buildings in Japanese gardens are mostly in quadrilateral form and use cleaner, straighter lines. Buildings tend to be shorter. Rather than compete for attention, they are more inclined to blend with the natural environment. Therefore, they do not have as powerful an aura as Chinese architecture, but they are exquisite and look more elegant.

Water bodies are mostly wide to emphasize vastness; therefore, most are lakes and larger ponds. Unlike Japanese gardens, Chinese gardens tend to make water bodies more attractive for visitors, such as short falls with rockeries instead of small streams. Lakes are often the central focus in Chinese gardens. These water forms usually come together with complex, exotic, and bold rockeries. Rockeries in Chinese gardens look very different from exquisite stones in Japanese gardens. They are sculptured as artificial mountains from individual stones in peculiar, porous shapes and symbolize a concentration of natural energy. Stones in Chinese gardens tend to imitate the forms of mountains as well as their imposing grandeur. This choice also reflects the Chinese pursuit of Shan Shui, which is translated to “mountain and water,” referring to landscape. 

Shot in the Humble Administrator's Garden, Suzhou, during my spring break, 2023

Similar to how Japanese gardens have strict rules in the placement of rocks, Chinese garden plants have clear meaning in particular locations for placement. For example, bamboos should be planted behind the house, osmanthus should grow in front of the hall, peonies in flower beds, parasols beside the staircase, and lilies and lotus should bloom in ponds. 

Shot in the Humble Administrator's Garden, Suzhou

Chinese gardens aim to express gloriousness. Designers consider how the placement of each object, far and close, creates beauty from any angle the viewer stands from. The composition of objects should imitate the appearance of falls and mountains in nature. Chinese gardens aim to create harmonious and aesthetic compositions from anywhere in the garden, therefore, designers pay great attention to the overall look and emotional expression of the landscape.

To Conclude

Garden is an individual’s interpretation towards nature. Due to differences in philosophy and religion, Chinese and Japanese gardens express varying emotions and meanings. The Chinese national aesthetics pursues the variety of architecture and livelihood, while Japanese aesthetics emphasizes wither and silence. Such differences shaped one to a captivating one and another to a simpler and exquisite one. As the garden designer Chen, Cong Zhou said, "Chinese gardens reveal nature within the artificial, while Japanese gardens reveal the artificial within nature." The highlights of Chinese gardens are how natural scenes, their essence, and their beauty are shown through the imitations of mountains and lakes. On the other hand, Japanese gardens convey the beauty of nature through the attention of artificial details and carefully planned designs using natural elements. Gardens, showing differences in how seemingly insignificant details are depicted creates dissimilar vibes, reflecting the uniqueness of nations and cultures.

Works Cited

Mehran, Carolyn. “Elements of a Japanese Garden.” The Blissful Gardeners, 12 May 2019, www.theblissfulgardeners.com/elements-of-a-japanese-garden/.

Pang, Kelly. “Chinese Gardens: History and Famous Historic Garden in China.” China Highlights, 31 Aug. 2021, www.chinahighlights.com/travelguide/architecture/history-garden.htm.

ShizenStyle. “The Main Differences between Chinese and Japanese Gardens.” ShizenStyle, 12 Mar. 2019, www.shizenstyle.com/post/the-main-differences-between-chinese-and-japanese-gardens.

Wall, Yasmin. “The Art of Japanese Gardens | Stories.” The Oxfordshire Gardener, 29 Apr. 2022, theoxfordshiregardener.co.uk/art-of-japanese-gardens/#:~:text=Chinese%20gardens%20traditionally%20are%20more. Accessed 16 July 2024.

Way, Simon. “All You Need to Know about Japanese Wood.” Daitool, 22 Apr. 2024, daitool.com/blogs/news/all-you-need-to-know-about-japanese-wood. Accessed 16 July 2024.

entry.title: Preservation or Operation?
entry.content:
A Powerful Tool

In a community, parks are green spaces that offer opportunities for recreation and are powerful tools to address critical urban infrastructure, health, and environmental issues vital to the well-being of communities. From expansive national parks that showcase nature's grandeur to neighborhood parks for respite, these areas foster a sense of community involvement while preserving the natural environment.

Different types of parks include urban parks, national parks, state parks, historical parks, water parks, amusement parks, town square parks, etc. Each type of park differs in its purpose of usage and way of management. For example, urban parks are located within a city or town and feature areas like sports fields, walking paths, picnic areas, and sometimes tourist spots like museums. National parks are often far from urban areas and offer a wide range of recreational activities like hiking, camping, and kayaking. These parks are large lands protected by the government for perseverance.

Yosamite, California, shot in August 2023 during my summer break

“Green Lungs”- Urban Parks

In urban planning, a park is the heart of a bustling city, serving as a sanctuary where nature and urban life harmoniously coexist. It has the power to enhance city growth and population health by encouraging active lifestyles and strengthening local economies through job creation. Parks also benefit the environment by controlling air pollution and reducing greenhouse gas emissions in cities. Urban parks provide public areas for hosting social events and engaging in physical activities. Their essence as public spaces intertwined with connections to nature is akin to Legos without instruction guides, offering numerous possible activities within the space.

Gaswork Park, Seattle, shot in June, 2024 during my summer break

Since parks are so crucial as the 'green lungs' of cities in creating a sustainable environment, the necessity of park protection is essential. By maintaining their cleanliness and safety, we ensure that parks continue to enliven the city. This is why, when discussing the management of parks, preservation often comes to mind. However, is preservation alone enough to keep them thriving?

Miyashita Park and Highline Park

Miyashita Park is a "three-dimensional" urban park located in Tokyo, Japan. Unlike most flatly spread urban parks, Miyashita has four floors, constructed in a long, narrow form. It is a combination of a mall, hotel, and park. Miyashita Park broke the traditional boundaries between parks and commercial facilities by creating a space where commerce and nature coexist. The four floors of the building comprise a mall providing abundant shopping and dining options. On the rooftop is the park, featuring a lawn, benches, and sports and entertainment facilities such as a rock climbing wall,volleyball court, and skateboard park. Unlike traditional parks, Miyashita Park is elevated above the busy streets of Shibuya. This integration of business and park space exemplifies how commercial value and recreational areas can coexist. Under such operation, the park has become not only a place to relax but also a commercial hub that attracts residents and tourists, eventually becoming a tourism spot.

Image source: “MIYASHITA PARK - Where to Shop, Access, Hours & Price.” GOOD LUCK TRIP, 7 Dec. 2023, www.gltjp.com/en/directory/item/15064/. Accessed 10 July 2024.

‌

Image source: “Miyashita Park by Nikken Sekkei | Parks.” Architonic, www.architonic.com/en/project/nikken-sekkei-miyashita-park/20250124. Accessed 10 July 2024.

‌High Line Park is an elevated trail constructed from an abandoned railway in New York. The park stretches above the city of Manhattan, covering a total length of 2.4 kilometers. Initially, the abandoned railroad was slated for removal by the New York government. However, an association named "Friends of the High Line" opposed the proposal and designed this park, protecting industrial cultural heritage while generating significant profit for neighboring communities. The park utilizes a unique strategy called "agri-tecture" (agriculture + architecture) for its paving system, allowing weeds to grow in the sidewalk fissures. Today, the High Line is a tourist destination and a public space that integrates the park area with the neighborhood, the street, and the city. Facilities include food courts, museums, markets, and playgrounds. Visitors can enjoy unique views of New York City along the green trail while experiencing art, performances, and socialization. Operating the High Line through the integration of arts, commerce, and nature demonstrates how a park can build connections between individuals and the community.

Image source: Grover, Spencer. “The High Line: A Transformative Urban Park in New York City.” ELIKA New York, 15 July 2023, www.elikarealestate.com/blog/the-high-line/.

Parks, as landscape architecture, play a vital role in building connection between individuals and the intrinsic beauty of life.

Balancing Preservation and Operation

Do the above cases tell us that maintenance is not as important as we thought? Certainly not; any operation strategy for parks is built on the basis of a clean and sustainable environment. However, with only maintenance, parks are more like exhibits for people to appreciate. In fact, a park becomes a burden due to the need for money and labor for protection. But with operation, it is an asset that can attract more residents and tourists. Operations do not necessarily have to be complex like how Miyashita Park and Highline Park run. It could be as simple as hosting performances, festivals, and social activities that raise interaction between individuals and the environment. These activities, which may seem insignificant, can in fact enhance involvement and belongingness in the community. Parks are not only for appreciation but also for individuals to feel the connection between themselves, the city, nature, and the world. With maintenance, we preserve their beauty. With operation, we keep them lively.

entry.title: Evaluating Architecture
entry.content:
In contemporary society, architecture is an indispensable part of our lives: we wake up in a building; when we walk out of our house, we are surrounded by architecture—our lives are intertwined with it. Today, architecture transcends mere cityscapes, embodying the aesthetic aspirations and life philosophies of individuals. When evaluating and critiquing architecture, we analyze its social implications and explore diverse perspectives and opinions that reflect our unique individuality.

How to Evaluate Architecture?

There is an "objective" method of evaluating the effectiveness of a building through the perspectives of Function, Economy, and Beauty (FEB). Function evaluation includes the measurement of safety, construction quality, sustainability, durability, comfort, and whether the architecture meets the client's requirements. Economic evaluation questions whether the money spent to achieve the desired functions is reasonable and minimized to its highest extent. The evaluation of beauty considers how aesthetically pleasing and attractive the architecture is to its audience while harmonizing with its surroundings. Although there is a set of rubrics and criteria to evaluate the effectiveness of architecture, different voices, opinions, and critiques towards a building are inevitable.

Oriental Pearl Tower- Shanghai, China

The Oriental Pearl Tower located in Shanghai, China, is an example of a controversial architecture. Standing by the river, the entire structure tends to picture an imagery of shining pearls. Undeniably, the Oriental Pearl Tower is a historical and significant symbol of China and Chinese culture, but there has been numerous debates about whether or not it is a “good” building.

Image source: “The Oriental Pearl Tower：The Landmark of Shanghai.” Www.chinaeducationaltours.com, www.chinaeducationaltours.com/guide/shanghai-the-oriental-pearl-tower.htm.

‌
“The top of the tower should offer the best view of Shanghai, but closer sites like the Bund are not visible.”* (“Shanghai Oriental Pearl Tower - Shanghai Attraction.” Www.chinaodysseytours.com, www.chinaodysseytours.com/shanghai/oriental-pearl-tower.html.)*

The evaluation of the function of the tower has been slightly controversial, but the most debatable criticism is its aesthetic concern.

“The styling is rustic, the color of concrete is dated.”

“An important aesthetic criterion for a city's skyline is that it is memorable, and in this light, the Oriental Pearl, which serves as the ‘captain’ of Lujiazui, is certainly a successful building.”(JJ Ying, Zhihu, December 21, 2021)

“Pearl of the Orient seemed advanced in concept at the time, but now it may look a bit oddly styled and not quite matching the Shanghai World Financing Center, Jinmao Tower and Shanghai Tower.” (Silentale, Zhihu, October 21, 2022)

“All I can associate it with is two meatballs on a skewer. The three-legged stand at the base of the tower is so confining and abrupt that it disrupts the rhythm of the whole building. It's the same effect as if an upright man had been standing very sparsely and then had a rope tied around his knees.” (Di, Wang, Zhihu, October 25, 2022)

Before researching for comments on the Pearl of Orient, I did not expect that its design raises debatable discussions among the public. As someone who has visited The Bund and the Pearl of Orient several times, I made more compliments than criticism regarding the aesthetics towards this symbol of Shanghai. The structure of Pearl of Orient stands out from Lujiazui. It emphasizes round shape while all other buildings show stiff straight lines, breaking down the rigid geometric combinations. Similarly, the use of bright red color adds an attraction to Lujiazui while all other architectures are in a cool, glass exterior. With the use of a representative color of China and round shape representing happiness among Chinese, these choice also highlights Chinese culture, shaping the architecture as a significant symbol of China. These factors discussed as criticisms by some voices are what I consider as elements that sculptured the Pearl of Orient as the postcard, an impression of China remembered by the world, which makes it a successful and effective architecture that has transcended the meaning it is initially bestowed with.

However, after considering the disagreements, I try to evaluate this architecture as if this is my first sight. Obviously, this is not possible, since the Pearl of Orient has created a deep and influencing impression on me. But it is true that some of its flaws are magnified, and the criticisms are now voices that remind me of reasons why this can be considered as an ineffective architecture. Now evaluating the architecture based on the mentioned criticisms, I understand that disregarding its surroundings and location, just merely looking at the tower does not set off its glamorous Chinese style. The bottom legs of the tower are slightly abrupt. However, afterall, they would not change my impression towards this architecture. I still consider the Pearl of Orient as a successful tower that represents Shanghai and China.

The change in my comments shows how varying perspectives influences one’s evaluation. Observing architecture from more than one angle can provide us with a more comprehensive judgment of a building, however, this does not tremendously change our impression and evaluation towards the architecture. Taking the Pearl of Orient as an example, my first impression of this building was formed when I was a kid, before I even learned its significance, influence and diverse commentaries. That is why, even after absorbing different opinions, my evaluation would not change, proving it to be a true and precise judgment from myself. What makes our evaluation pure and accurate, is that there are no other factors to consider, other than one’s intuitive point of view.

Today, there are so many voices that distract us from feeling the true impressions from architecture. Information from the media tends to give us an evaluation of a building before we develop our own judgments, setting stereotypes of what is “good.” These information eliminates our development of realistic evaluations and forms vague impressions. Although there are criterias for evaluating architecture, what weighs more are the unique impressions and intuitive emotions we develop. To practice forming our purest and most precise judgments, begin by understanding and confronting the feelings we perceive from our homes and surroundings. By building and listening to our own opinions on our lives, we build confidence in understanding architecture. Prioritize personal impressions, emotions, and memories as the foundation of our judgments rather than relying solely on media messages. Or, go ahead to personally experience and observe architecture with unaided eyes, immersing ourself in the reality of the built environment.

entry.title: Welcome to My Website!
entry.content:
Biao ti yi

Biao ti er

Biao ti san

lalala

damn

momo and nayeon

E=mc2E = mc^2E=mc2
